record_office_terminology.land_records: Register of Deeds (deeds, mortgages, liens -- one office per county)
record_office_terminology.probate: Probate Court (wills, estates, guardianships -- formerly County Judge; also called Register in Probate in some periods)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (marriage licenses from territorial period; state vital records registration began 1907 with sporadic earlier county-level compliance)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1907 (statewide birth and death registration mandated; earlier vital events found in church records, WHS collections, and newspapers; some counties have earlier county-level records dating to 1870s-1890s)

burned_county_authority: Wisconsin Historical Society (WHS), Madison -- Wisconsin has no significant burned counties. Record survival is excellent across all 72 counties due to stable governance, the WHS's extraordinary centralized microfilming and preservation program (one of the oldest and most comprehensive state historical societies in the nation, founded 1846), and the absence of Civil War or major catastrophic courthouse losses. The Great Peshtigo Fire of 1871 affected parts of Marinette, Oconto, and Door counties but did not destroy county courthouses. WHS holds microfilm copies of county records from all 72 counties.
administrative_notes: Wisconsin Territory was organized in 1836 and admitted to the Union as the 30th state on May 29, 1848. Previously part of the Northwest Territory (1787), Indiana Territory (1800), Illinois Territory (1809), and Michigan Territory (1818). Wisconsin is a public land state -- original federal land transfers are documented in BLM/GLO patents available online at glorecords.blm.gov. The Wisconsin Historical Society (WHS) in Madison, founded in 1846, is one of the nation's premier historical societies and the central genealogical repository for the state. WHS holdings include microfilmed county records from all 72 counties, the nation's fourth-largest newspaper collection, manuscript collections, maps, and photographs. Record offices: Register of Deeds (land records), County Clerk (marriage licenses, vital records), Probate Court (wills, estates, guardianships). Wisconsin conducted state censuses in 1855, 1865, 1875, 1885, 1895, and 1905 -- all at WHS, and they are unusually detailed (including birthplace, occupation, and citizenship). Native American nations with reservations include Ojibwe/Chippewa (six bands), Menominee, Potawatomi, Ho-Chunk (Winnebago), Oneida, Stockbridge-Munsee, and Brothertown. Wisconsin has very strong German, Norwegian, Swedish, Polish, Finnish, Danish, Bohemian (Czech), Luxembourger, Belgian (Walloon), Swiss, Dutch, and Irish immigrant communities. The lumber industry dominated northern Wisconsin from the 1850s-1920s. Brewing (Milwaukee) and dairy farming were major industries. The Iron Brigade (2nd, 6th, 7th Wisconsin Infantry) was one of the most famous Civil War units.

core_repositories.description: Wisconsin's state historical society and the single most important repository for genealogical research in Wisconsin. Founded in 1846, WHS is one of the oldest and most comprehensive state historical societies in the nation. Holdings include microfilmed county records from all 72 counties (deeds, probate, vital records, court records), the nation's fourth-largest newspaper collection, state censuses (1855-1905), manuscript collections, maps, photographs, and military records. The WHS Library-Archives in Madison is open to the public. The WHS also operates historic sites and area research centers at UW System campuses across the state.

core_repositories.repository: Wisconsin Historical Society Area Research Centers at UW campuses statewide
core_repositories.description: Network of 14 Area Research Centers at University of Wisconsin campuses across the state, each holding county and local records for their designated region. ARCs hold original county records, local government records, manuscript collections, business records, and organizational records deposited by counties and communities. Each ARC serves a multi-county region. This decentralized network makes research possible without traveling to Madison.

core_repositories.source_name: NARA -- National Archives at Chicago (Great Lakes Region)
core_repositories.description: Federal records for Wisconsin including federal court records (naturalizations, bankruptcy), military records (Fort Howard, Fort Crawford, Camp Randall), Bureau of Indian Affairs records for Wisconsin tribes (Ojibwe, Menominee, Potawatomi, Ho-Chunk, Oneida, Stockbridge-Munsee), BLM land office records, and US Census originals.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Wisconsin county records (deeds, probate, marriages), vital records, church records, and cemetery records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at WHS and county offices. Key collections include Wisconsin marriages, probate records, state censuses, and vital records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability guides, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Wisconsin's unique record types (state censuses, ethnic church records) and provides research strategies for the state's diverse immigrant communities.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Wisconsin newspapers. WHS has the nation's fourth-largest newspaper collection. Includes German-language newspapers (Milwaukee Seebote, Germania), Norwegian-language papers, Polish-language papers, and English-language county weeklies and city dailies. Legal notices, obituaries, estate advertisements, and community news.

The WHS newspaper collection is the fourth-largest in the US. Holdings include thousands of Wisconsin newspaper titles on microfilm and in original format. German-, Norwegian-, Polish-, and other foreign-language newspapers are particularly valuable for immigrant community research. Newspapers.com and GenealogyBank have indexed some Wisconsin papers.

military_records.description: WHS holds Wisconsin adjutant general records, Civil War service records (Iron Brigade -- 2nd, 6th, 7th Wisconsin Infantry), militia records, and veterans' records. The Iron Brigade was one of the most famous Union units of the Civil War. WWI and WWII draft registrations at NARA. Wisconsin contributed approximately 91,000 soldiers to the Civil War. Camp Randall in Madison was a training camp and POW facility.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- Wisconsin
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Wisconsin soldiers. Wisconsin contributed approximately 91,000 Union soldiers. The Iron Brigade (2nd, 6th, 7th Wisconsin, plus 19th Indiana and 24th Michigan) is extensively documented.

land_and_property.source_name: Wisconsin Land Records -- County Registers / WHS / BLM GLO
land_and_property.repository: County Register of Deeds offices / Wisconsin Historical Society, Madison / BLM GLO
land_and_property.description: Wisconsin is a public land state -- original federal land transfers are documented in BLM/GLO patents available online. Each county has a Register of Deeds office maintaining land records. WHS holds microfilm copies of historical deed books from many counties. Earliest land records in Brown and Crawford counties date from 1818. Lead mining district land records (southwestern Wisconsin) include mining claims and mineral rights.

vital_records.source_name: Wisconsin Vital Records -- WHS / Department of Health Services
vital_records.repository: Wisconsin Historical Society, Madison / Wisconsin Department of Health Services
vital_records.description: Statewide vital records registration mandated in 1907, but some counties have earlier records (1870s-1890s). WHS holds historical vital records indexes and some original records. Modern vital records from the Wisconsin Department of Health Services. For pre-registration vital events, researchers must use church records, cemetery records, newspaper notices, and county-level records. FamilySearch has indexed some Wisconsin vital records.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Wisconsin Church Records -- WHS / Denominational Archives
church_and_cemetery.repository: Wisconsin Historical Society / Catholic Archdiocese of Milwaukee / Lutheran synod archives / local congregations
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are essential for pre-vital registration research in Wisconsin. Catholic Archdiocese of Milwaukee and dioceses of Green Bay, La Crosse, Madison, and Superior hold parish registers. Lutheran church records (Missouri Synod, Wisconsin Synod, Norwegian Synod, Augustana Synod) through denominational archives. Norwegian Lutheran records at NAHA (St. Olaf College). German, Polish, Czech, and other ethnic church records often in original languages. WHS has transcribed and microfilmed some church records. Dutch Reformed, Moravian, Methodist, Congregational, and other denominational records also available.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Wisconsin. DAR chapters, county genealogical societies, and the WHS have published cemetery transcription volumes for many counties. Many church cemeteries, municipal cemeteries, and pioneer burial grounds have been documented. Coverage is growing.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society publishing the WSGS Newsletter and conducting conferences and workshops. Maintains links to county and regional genealogical societies across Wisconsin. Sponsors research and publication projects.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all 72 Wisconsin counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, and research guides.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Wisconsin has no extinct counties but several renamed ones. Dallas County was renamed Barron County in 1869. Bad Ax County was renamed Vernon County in 1862 (the name 'Bad Ax' was considered undesirable after the Battle of Bad Axe during the Black Hawk War of 1832). New County was renamed Langlade County in 1880. Gates County was renamed Rusk County in 1905. Researchers should search under both original and current county names at WHS. La Pointe County was an unorganized territory that was divided into several northern counties. Brown and Crawford were the original two counties (1818, under Michigan Territory), and most Wisconsin counties descend from them.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Wisconsin Native American Records -- NARA / WHS / Tribal Archives
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago) / Wisconsin Historical Society, Madison / Tribal offices
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Wisconsin has eleven federally recognized tribal nations: six Ojibwe/Chippewa bands (Bad River, Lac Courte Oreilles, Lac du Flambeau, Red Cliff, St. Croix, Sokaogon/Mole Lake), Menominee, Forest County Potawatomi, Ho-Chunk (Winnebago), Oneida, and Stockbridge-Munsee (Mohican). Also the Brothertown Indians. NARA Great Lakes Region holds BIA agency records, tribal enrollment, allotment records, annuity rolls, and census rolls. WHS holds some Native American manuscript collections. The removal of Potawatomi, Ho-Chunk, and other nations in the 1830s-1850s is documented at NARA. Treaty records (Treaty of the Cedars 1836, Treaty of La Pointe 1842/1854) established reservations.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Wisconsin Immigration and Naturalization Records -- NARA / WHS / County Courts
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago) / Wisconsin Historical Society, Madison / County courts
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Wisconsin attracted massive European immigration: German (the largest group), Norwegian, Swedish, Polish, Finnish, Danish, Bohemian (Czech), Luxembourger, Belgian (Walloon), Swiss, Dutch, Irish, Cornish, Welsh, and Italian immigrants. Naturalization records filed in county circuit courts are at WHS Area Research Centers and NARA. Port of Milwaukee records. Milwaukee immigration records at NARA. Ship passenger lists for Great Lakes ports. Ethnic community organizations (Turnverein, Sons of Norway, Polish National Alliance) documented at WHS. German-language, Norwegian-language, and Polish-language newspaper records at WHS.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Wisconsin has no burned counties. Record survival across all 72 counties is excellent. The Wisconsin Historical Society (WHS) in Madison has conducted one of the most comprehensive centralized preservation programs in the nation, microfilming county records from all 72 counties. The Great Peshtigo Fire of October 8, 1871 (the deadliest wildfire in US history, coinciding with the Great Chicago Fire) affected parts of Marinette, Oconto, Door, Brown, and Kewaunee counties but did not destroy county courthouses. Some early local records in the affected area were lost, but county-level records were preserved.
